title: CustomPainter
objectives: Understand CustomPainter via a real-life analogy
Draw shapes, paths, gradients, and text on a Canvas
Implement `shouldRepaint` correctly to avoid wasted paints
Combine with animations for smooth custom visuals

sections.markdown:
**Think of a **blank cardboard sign** and a **stack of markers.**

- Flutter hands you a `Canvas` and a `Size`: 'here's a blank rectangle 300 by 200 pixels. Draw whatever you want.'
- **`Paint`** is your marker: pick a color, a stroke width, anti-aliased or not, gradient fill or solid.
- **`Path`** is what you'd sketch first in pencil — a line from A to B to C, a curve, an arc — before you outline in marker.
- **`Canvas`** exposes the drawing verbs — `drawLine`, `drawCircle`, `drawPath`, `drawArc`, `drawImage`, `drawText`. Save/restore + rotate/translate to build compositions.
- **`shouldRepaint`** is your judgement 'do I need to redraw this sign?' — return `true` only when a property that AFFECTS THE DRAWING has actually changed. Return `false` and Flutter reuses the last picture — free performance.

**When to reach for CustomPainter:** anything that isn't a stock widget — signature capture, charts, custom progress rings, particle effects, waveforms, radar sweeps, watermarks, thermometer bars. Once you can draw with `Canvas`, entire UI categories open up.

**`CustomPainter`** is Flutter's escape hatch to direct 2D drawing. You extend it, implement `paint(canvas, size)` and `shouldRepaint(oldDelegate)`, then feed it to a `CustomPaint` widget. Inside `paint`, you call into a `Canvas` (drawLine, drawPath, drawImage, drawText, ...) with `Paint` objects (color, stroke, gradient). Repaints are optimized via `shouldRepaint` — return true only when drawing-affecting props changed.
## The four things you'll use every time

rows: The drawing surface (methods: drawLine, drawCircle, drawPath, drawText...)
How to draw: color, stroke width, style, shader, blend mode
A sequence of moves/lines/curves you can then fill or stroke

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — a progress ring + smiley + wave
## Combining with animation for smooth custom visuals
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Returning `true` from `shouldRepaint` unconditionally.** Flutter repaints every frame even when nothing changed. Compare only the fields that affect drawing.
**Mistake 2: Allocating `Paint`s / `Path`s inside `paint()`.** `paint()` runs at 60 fps. Cache them as fields on the painter or as `late final` in `initState`.
**Mistake 3: Not wrapping animated painters in `RepaintBoundary`.** Every frame invalidates the ancestor's layer, re-recording neighbors unnecessarily.
**Mistake 4: Doing heavy computation in `paint()`.** Precompute `Path`s and offsets in the constructor or on prop change; `paint()` should be nearly-pure canvas calls.
**Mistake 5: Ignoring `size` and hard-coding pixels.** Use `size.width`, `size.height`, `size.center(Offset.zero)`. Makes the painter responsive.
## Best practices
**1. Compare **only drawing-relevant** props in `shouldRepaint`.**
**2. Precompute `Path`s once** when input changes — reuse in `paint`.
**3. Wrap animated `CustomPaint` in `RepaintBoundary`.**
**4. Use `Path.combine` / `Path.transform`** for compositional drawing.
**5. For text, use `TextPainter`.** Never assume text has a fixed size; call `layout()` first.
**6. Consider a shader / Rive / Lottie for very complex visuals.** CustomPainter is powerful but Flutter's shader system or Rive can outperform hand-drawn 2D at scale.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Draw a circle** filled and stroked; play with `strokeWidth` and `strokeCap`.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Progress ring.** Build the sample; drive `progress` with an AnimationController.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Signature pad.** Capture drag events, append to a `Path`, redraw.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Line chart** from a `List<double>`; label axes; auto-scale.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Animated waveform** with a smooth sine curve driven by an AnimationController.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is `CustomPainter` and when do you use it?**

**A:** `CustomPainter` is Flutter's low-level 2D drawing API. You extend it, implement `paint(canvas, size)` (draw whatever you want on the `Canvas`) and `shouldRepaint(oldDelegate)` (decide when to redraw), and feed it to a `CustomPaint` widget. Use it when the design isn't achievable with standard widgets: signature capture, charts, progress rings, waveforms, particle effects, custom badges, watermarks. For very complex visuals with many pieces, consider `Rive` (interactive vector animations) or shaders instead — but `CustomPainter` is the workhorse for one-off custom 2D.

**Q2: Why is `shouldRepaint` important?**

**A:** `paint()` can run at 60fps (or more). Flutter calls `shouldRepaint(oldDelegate)` to decide whether the CANVAS RECORDING needs to be redone. If you return `false`, Flutter reuses the last recorded picture — **free performance**. If you return `true` unconditionally, you paint every frame even when nothing changed. **Correct implementation:** compare the fields on `this` (the new delegate) against `oldDelegate`; return `true` only if a field that AFFECTS the drawing has changed. `bool shouldRepaint(_MyPainter old) => old.progress != progress || old.color != color;`

**Q3: What's the difference between `paint()` running and the pipeline repainting?**

**A:** `CustomPainter.paint()` records drawing commands into a `Picture`. The picture becomes the content of the `CustomPaint`'s RenderObject's layer. The layer is composited by the raster thread onto the GPU. So `paint()` running fast doesn't magically produce pixels — those are the raster thread's job. But `paint()` is on the UI thread; slow `paint()` is UI-thread jank. **Optimizations for `paint()`:** cache Paints / Paths, minimize allocations, avoid heavy math. **Optimizations for the raster side:** wrap in `RepaintBoundary` (isolates the layer), use simple Paint modes (avoid `saveLayer` unless needed).

**Q4: When should you use `Path` vs multiple draw calls?**

**A:** A `Path` batches related drawing into ONE draw call. If you're drawing many segments that will be filled or stroked together, build a `Path` and call `canvas.drawPath(path, paint)` once. Cheaper than many `canvas.drawLine` calls. Also enables `Path.combine`, `Path.transform`, `Path.contains(point)` — great for hit-testing. **Use multiple draw calls** for independent shapes with different paints or when the shapes come from different data — a path can only carry one paint at a time. Rule: same paint → path; different paints → separate calls.

**Q6: How do you handle taps inside a CustomPainter?**

**A:** Wrap the `CustomPaint` in a `GestureDetector` and translate the tap coordinates to painter coordinates. Then use `Path.contains(offset)` to test which shape was hit. Example: a pie chart with clickable slices — each slice is a `Path`, and on tap you iterate the slice-paths and find the containing one. **Alternative:** override `CustomPainter.hitTest(Offset position)` — Flutter calls it during hit-testing; return `true` if the position is inside your custom shape. This is more sophisticated than a whole-widget `GestureDetector` because you get slice-level (not rect-level) hit-testing.

**Q7: What's the difference between `CustomPainter` and a shader?**

**A:** **`CustomPainter`** — Dart-side 2D drawing. You issue commands to the Canvas from Dart; each frame's paint is recorded and rasterized. Good for complex vector shapes, dynamic geometry, hit-testable custom UI. **`Shader` / `ImageShader` / `FragmentProgram`** — GPU-side program written in a shading language (recent Flutter supports SPIR-V fragment shaders). Runs on the GPU per-pixel. Blazing fast; ideal for effects (blur, distortion, glow), image processing, procedural graphics. **Rule:** shapes → CustomPainter; per-pixel effects → shader. Shaders require more setup and shader-specific knowledge (GLSL-like syntax) but massively outperform `CustomPainter` for pixel-level work.

## Real-world usage

- **Progress rings, circular meters** — CustomPainter with an arc.
- **Line/bar/pie charts** — the `fl_chart` package uses CustomPainter internally.
- **Signature pads, drawing apps** — capture drag events → append to Path.
- **Waveforms / audio visualizers** — animated CustomPainter.
- **Custom badges, ribbons, notification dots** — geometric shapes.
- **Onboarding illustrations** — either CustomPainter, Rive, or Lottie.
- **Watermarks** — a full-screen CustomPainter overlaid over content.

## Advanced notes

- **`saveLayer` / `restore`** — offscreen layer for blend modes and clipping; expensive.
- **`Canvas.clipPath`** — clip subsequent drawing to a path.
- **`Canvas.rotate`, `Canvas.translate`, `Canvas.scale`** — transform composition; wrap in `save/restore`.
- **`Canvas.drawImage` / `drawImageRect`** — blit images into a painter.
- **`ui.PictureRecorder`** — record commands without a widget for programmatic image generation.
- **`FragmentProgram`** — SPIR-V shaders (Impeller only).
- **`Path.combine(Op.union, a, b)`** — union / intersect / subtract paths.
- **`Paint.imageFilter`, `Paint.maskFilter`** — blurs and other Skia-side filters.
- **`Paint.blendMode`** — Porter-Duff blending.
- **`Canvas.drawShadow`** — cheap shadow rendering.
- **`Semantics` widgets around `CustomPaint`** — accessibility annotations.

Return true only when drawing-affecting props changed.
Use TextPainter — create → layout() → paint(canvas, offset).
RepaintBoundary around animated CustomPaint isolates repaints.
Build a Path and drawPath once instead of many draw calls.
GestureDetector + Path.contains(offset), or override hitTest in the painter.

challenges.text: Draw a filled + stroked circle; try strokeCap.round vs square.
Progress ring using drawArc + LinearGradient shader.
Animated ring driven by an AnimationController (repeat).
Draw a smiley with drawCircle + Path curves + TextPainter percent.
Signature pad: capture drag Offsets, append to a Path, redraw.
Simple line chart from List<double>; auto-scale axes.
Animated waveform — sine curve driven by controller, wrapped in RepaintBoundary.
